author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
’From Him’ And ‘Of Him’ Do Not Mean The Same Thing.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: ’From Him’ And ‘Of Him’ Do Not Mean The Same Thing.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But "from Him" does not mean the same as "of Him."

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For what is of Him may be said to be from Him; but not everything that is from Him is rightly said to be of Him.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For from Him are heaven and earth, because He made them; but not of Him because they are not of His substance.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: As in the case of a man who begets a son and makes a house, from himself is the son, from himself is the house, but the son is of him, the house is of earth and wood.

verses.book: Concerning the Nature of Good, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But this is so, because as a man he cannot make something even of nothing; but God of whom are all things, through whom are all things, in whom are all things, had no need of any material which He had not made to assist His omnipotence.
